title: Reverse Integer
statement: Given a signed 32-bit integer x, return the integer formed by reversing its decimal digits. Preserve the sign. If the reversed value would be outside the signed 32-bit range [-2^31, 2^31 - 1], return 0. The solution must avoid storing a 64-bit intermediate value.
constraints: x is in the signed 32-bit range: -2^31 <= x <= 2^31 - 1. Because 64-bit integers are unavailable, the algorithm must detect overflow before doing a multiply-by-10-and-add step that could exceed 32-bit bounds.

brute_force.approach: Convert x to a string, peel off a leading '-' if present, reverse the remaining characters, parse the result back to an integer with the original sign, then check whether it is inside the 32-bit signed range. This is simple and correct in languages where parsing the reversed string cannot overflow first, but it violates the spirit of the no-64-bit-intermediate constraint in many environments.
brute_force.time: O(d), where d is the number of decimal digits

optimal.approach: Build the reversed value one digit at a time using arithmetic. Repeatedly remove the last digit from x and prepare to append it to the result. Before computing result * 10 + digit, compare result against the largest or smallest safe prefix so the operation is only performed when it is guaranteed to remain inside the signed 32-bit range.
optimal.time: O(d), where d is the number of decimal digits

key_insight: You can know whether appending the next digit would overflow by checking the current reversed prefix before multiplying it by 10.
ladder: try string reversal or full numeric rebuild
append one last digit at a time
find the last safe prefix before appending
handle positive and negative limits separately

traps.wrong_approach: Reverse the whole number first and then check whether it is in range.
traps.why_wrong: The reversed value itself may overflow before the check in an environment limited to 32-bit integers.
traps.counterexample: x = 1534236469; the mathematical reverse is 9646324351, which is outside the 32-bit signed range.

traps.wrong_approach: Use the same final-digit cutoff for both positive and negative overflow.
traps.why_wrong: The signed 32-bit range is asymmetric: the maximum ends in 7, while the minimum ends in -8 when building with signed digits.
traps.counterexample: x = -8463847412 is not a valid input, but during reversal-style reasoning the boundary value -2147483648 is valid while 2147483648 is not; treating both sides as ending at 7 rejects or accepts the wrong boundary.

traps.wrong_approach: Strip the sign, reverse abs(x), then reapply the sign without thinking about the minimum integer.
traps.why_wrong: In many 32-bit languages, abs(-2147483648) cannot be represented as a positive 32-bit integer.
traps.counterexample: x = -2147483648; computing abs(x) overflows in a strict 32-bit signed environment.

traps.wrong_approach: Forget that trailing zeroes disappear in the integer result.
traps.why_wrong: Reversal produces an integer, not a fixed-width digit string, so leading zeroes after reversal are not kept.

underlying_primitive: Reasoning about whether a small arithmetic update will cross a fixed numeric boundary before performing the update
